categories.summary: A new multicenter study of critically ill ischemic stroke patients used early patterns of six vital signs to define three physiologic “phenotypes” — tachycardic‑tachypneic, hypertensive‑stable, and low‑diastolic quiescent — finding the tachycardic‑tachypneic group had much higher short‑ and long‑term mortality, and that phenotype-based models outperformed traditional scores and hinted that fluids and sedatives may need to be tailored by phenotype. In multiple sclerosis, a small 12‑week trial of repeated simulated high‑altitude sessions (intermittent hypoxia) was safe, feasible, and suggested possible functional benefits, supporting larger controlled trials. And a large Medicaid claims study found women with epilepsy were slightly more likely to receive contraception while those with intellectual and developmental disabilities were much less likely, with co‑occurring conditions changing which methods were prescribed — a signal of inequities in reproductive care that needs attention. Together, these findings push neurology toward more personalized ICU care, novel therapeutic exploration in MS, and better attention to reproductive health disparities.

Today’s cardiology highlights: A new machine‑learning tool called CONFIDENT uses routine clinical and imaging data to better predict death and heart‑failure hospitalizations in patients with HFpEF, outperforming established risk scores and offering a way to personalize care and trial selection. In atrial fibrillation research, targeted sonodynamic therapy reduced inflammation and fibrosis in epicardial fat in animal models, with single‑cell and genetic evidence (linking CXCL8 to AF risk) supporting that dampening TLR4/NF‑κB signaling could be a novel, tissue‑specific treatment strategy. And a real‑world “natural experiment” from a device recall showed that temporarily switching patients used to dual‑chamber pacing into non‑synchronous ventricular pacing caused rapid heart‑failure deterioration and only partial recovery, emphasizing that preserving atrioventricular synchrony is vital for clinical stability.

A long-term study of Australian dairy cows shows the Q fever bacterium often appears in placentas at calving and can re-emerge in milk by mid-lactation; intriguingly, specific immune profiles—higher early antibody levels without a strong cell-mediated response—were linked to later milk shedding, pointing to intermittent herd-level risks to farmworkers and consumers. In the United States, childhood coverage for the seven core vaccines has inched up from about 71% to 77% since 2010, but persistent gaps tied to insurance, income, language, and region leave vulnerable pockets that can spark outbreaks. On the treatment front, new “third-generation” photosensitizers and nanodelivery approaches are making photodynamic therapy more targeted and may extend its use to infectious diseases, although challenges like limited light penetration and low tissue oxygen still restrict broader clinical use. Together, these studies highlight how biological, social, and technological factors all shape our ability to prevent and control infectious diseases.

Researchers analyzed anal HPV infections and related lesions in 1,178 HIV-negative young women enrolled in a substudy of the Costa Rica HPV Vaccine Trial, collecting anal swabs and cytology over multiple visits and referring participants for high-resolution anoscopy when results were abnormal or when HPV types 16/18/45 were detected. The cohort—selected to enrich for anal disease—included women with prevalent anal HPV16 and other high‑ and low‑risk HPV types, and over a median follow-up of nearly six years just 6% (71 women) were referred for further anoscopic evaluation. This work creates a rare, well-characterized natural history dataset for anal HPV in women without HIV, filling an important gap as anal cancer rates rise and helping guide future prevention, screening, and vaccine impact studies.

Researchers report three practical advances: a novel sonodynamic therapy reduced macrophage-driven inflammation in the fat around the heart in animal models of atrial fibrillation and, backed by single-cell data and genetics, points to the inflammatory chemokine CXCL8 (IL‑8) as a treatable driver — suggesting a targeted way to prevent or ease AF. A case report warns that spontaneous coronary artery dissection can strike otherwise healthy young women after induced vaginal delivery, reminding clinicians that labor can trigger life‑threatening heart attacks even without classic risk factors. And a real‑world study found that regular dairy intake commonly disrupts levothyroxine absorption, often forcing dose changes and longer stabilization, so simple timing and dietary counselling could improve care and cut costs.

A new study in immunocompromised adults shows that probe-capture targeted next‑generation sequencing (ptNGS) markedly boosts detection of lung pathogens compared with standard microbiology: ptNGS and unbiased mNGS each found causative organisms in about 80% of cases versus only ~27% for conventional tests, and ptNGS agreed with mNGS on about 92% of findings. However, sequencing was less specific and often picked up viruses or opportunistic organisms in patients without true infection, so results must be interpreted carefully to avoid overtreatment. The researchers also found that infected lungs had lower microbial diversity and a shifted community composition, suggesting infection-related changes in the lung microbiome that could inform future diagnostics or treatments. Overall, ptNGS looks like a promising, more sensitive tool for diagnosing difficult lung infections in vulnerable patients, but clinical judgment remains essential.

Two new reports point to clearer ways to prevent and treat common bone problems. A systematic review found that artificial intelligence and radiomics applied to CT/MRI can markedly improve osteoporosis diagnosis and fracture-risk prediction (many models showed strong accuracy), raising the possibility of earlier, more precise intervention—though larger multicenter studies are still needed before routine clinical use. A separate prospective study of hemiepiphysiodesis for valgus/varus knee deformities in children showed high long-term satisfaction, reduced pain, stable activity levels, and few complications, supporting the procedure as an effective, well-tolerated alignment correction. Together, these findings promise better prediction of fracture risk and reliable surgical options to restore function and reduce pain.

New lab work in a worm model suggests drugs that stabilize microtubules can reduce seizures caused by CAMSAP gene defects, pointing to a possible targeted treatment path for a rare pediatric neurodevelopmental disorder. A comparative microanatomy study of polydactyly and the pediatric nail unit questions some assumptions about the onychodermis/onychodroblast model and could change how scientists study congenital nail problems. And in pediatric spine surgery, most kids who showed worrying intraoperative neurophysiology changes did not consistently meet individualized blood‑pressure (MAP) targets in the first 24 hours after surgery, revealing a modifiable care gap that may influence neurologic outcomes.

Recent work suggests aging’s tempo may be more fixed than we thought: improvements in lifespan come largely from delaying the start of age‑related decline rather than slowing the underlying process. In lab animals, a polysaccharide from the plant Eurycoma longifolia extended both lifespan and healthspan by boosting conserved stress‑response pathways and rebalancing metabolism, pointing to a low‑toxicity candidate for future nutraceuticals. For reproductive aging, studies reinforce that oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction are central causes of oocyte decline, and that antioxidants, lifestyle changes, and mitochondria‑targeted strategies could help preserve fertility. Together, these findings steer aging research toward delaying onset, strengthening cellular defenses, and protecting mitochondria to maintain function with age.

A large analysis of ~853,000 X (Twitter) posts from 2009–2023 shows public attention to ADHD and autism has risen, with English ADHD posts—and their engagement—peaking around the COVID-19 period, while Spanish posts contained more polarized personal stories and less trivialization. This social-media snapshot matters because it reveals real-time beliefs about causes and treatability that can inform targeted education and outreach. A separate scoping review across 15 Latin American countries finds racism is pervasive in health care—through policies, interpersonal mistreatment, and internalized stigma—leading to delayed diagnoses and poorer mental-health care for Indigenous, Afrodescendant, and migrant groups. Together, the findings underscore that both public perceptions and structural discrimination shape mental health outcomes and point to the need for culturally informed communication and systemic reforms.
